title: UK Inflation Jumps to 3.3% as Iran War Drives Fuel Price Surge, Pressuring Bank of England
detail: UK CPI rose to 3.3% in April 2026, driven by soaring fuel prices as the Iran war continued to disrupt global oil supply chains and feed through to consumer prices. The acceleration — coming just days after WTI crude rebounded to ~$95.75/bbl — adds significant pressure on the Bank of England, which faces the same stagflation dilemma confronting the Federal Reserve and ECB: inflation re-accelerating while growth risks mount. Fuel costs are the primary transmission channel: higher oil prices raised petrol prices and domestic energy costs, while supply-chain pass-through effects are broadening to food and goods. The Bank of England, which had been signaling a cautious easing bias, must now weigh whether to hold rates steady despite recession risk or tighten further to contain the second-order inflationary effects of the Iran war's energy shock. The UK figure aligns with the global pattern: euro-area HICP has also re-accelerated, US CPI (March) was 3.3%, and the IEA confirmed March 2026 saw the largest oil supply disruption in history at 10.1 mbpd. With the FOMC meeting April 29-30 and the ECB April 30, the Bank of England faces a pivotal window in which all major central banks must simultaneously navigate energy-driven inflation and slowing demand.

media.caption: UK inflation jumps to 3.3% as Iran war-driven fuel price surge feeds through to consumer prices
